fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small toolkit for our container auto-update agent. Basically the agent needs to figure out which containers to act on, how to talk to the registry, and how to load its own config. Right now every integration is doing this differently and we keep getting bugs where some containers get updated when they shouldn't, or credentials get handled wrong.

For the container picking logic, we need something like what we did with the opt-in/opt-out stuff in the billing service — same idea where some containers say 'yes update me' and others say 'no skip me', and we need to handle both modes plus a name-based allowlist and grouping by scope. There's also a weird edge case with leading characters on container names that keeps biting us, similar to the path handling issue we fixed in the storage module.

For registry auth, we need to encode credentials from environment variables into whatever token format the registry expects, and also figure out which server to talk to just from an image name.

For config, secrets should be loadable either inline or from a file on disk (ops team keeps asking for this), and we need to translate our connection settings into the right env vars for the Docker client. Default socket path should just work out of the box with no config.

The whole thing should be clean and composable — we want to wire these pieces together in different combinations depending on deployment mode.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the name allow-list, we do want the match to succeed not just on the exact reported name, but also when the target matches that reported name with its very first character removed. This is specifically to tolerate cases where the runtime gives us something like '/test' and the target is 'test'. Same deal as the annoying leading separator issue we’ve seen before. Also, if there are no target names configured at all, the name selector should impose no restriction and just fall back to the admit-all base behavior. In other words, an empty allow-list should not reject everything.

Also on the container predicates, the self check should only admit something when is_self_agent is explicitly true. If that flag is missing or just defaults to false, that candidate should not pass the self predicate.

For the composed selector behavior, the ordering matters. The disabled_label check is always first, before name matching, before any enable_label requirement, and before scope. If something explicitly opts out, it’s out immediately. That means a container can still be rejected even if its name is in the allow-list. In particular, a container whose enable marker is present with value=false is immediately rejected without consulting any other layer. On the flip side, for enable_label mode, admission is purely about presence of the marker, not its boolean value. If the marker exists and value=false, it should still be admitted; if the marker is absent, it should be rejected. Related to that, in build mode, enable_label only becomes a layer when enable_label is false says it should be included, and scope only becomes a layer when scope is not an empty string. The only layers that are always there are the opt-out exclusion first and then the name allow-list.

Just calling out too that this is meant to line up with Features 1.2 and 1.3 exactly: enable_label mode admits only when the marker is present and the value is irrelevant, while disabled_label mode rejects only when the marker is present and value is false. And for Feature 1.1 name matching, yes, we do want the comparison to succeed if the target matches the candidate name with its first character removed, so leading '/' on the reported name doesn’t break selection.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: filter(mode, containers, names=[], enable_label=False, scope='') -> List[Tuple[str, bool]]

trigger_keywords: credential token format
base64 encoding of credentials
JSON field order in auth token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The encoded credential token must be the Base64 encoding of a compact JSON document (no extra whitespace) with exactly two fields in this order: 'username' first, then 'password'. No other fields may be present. For example, user='user-a' and pass='pass-a' must produce the Base64 of '{"username":"user-a","password":"pass-a"}'.

trigger_keywords: missing credentials error output
empty user or pass result
both credentials must be present
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If either the username or the password is missing (empty string or not provided), no token should be produced. Instead the output must be the line 'error=missing_credentials'. Both fields must be non-empty for a token to be generated.

trigger_keywords: server extracted from image reference
registry host from image name
substring up to first slash
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The server address is extracted by parsing the image reference and taking everything up to the first path separator. For 'example.com/lib/app' the server is 'example.com'. For a reference whose leading component is an organization name (no dot/colon host), the organization name itself is returned as the server. An empty string or any input that cannot be parsed must produce 'error=invalid_reference'.

trigger_keywords: default docker host value
no host option supplied fallback
built-in socket address
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no host option is supplied, the docker_host must default to the built-in Unix socket address 'unix:///var/run/docker.sock'. When tlsverify is not set or false, docker_tls_verify must be output as an empty string (not omitted). When tlsverify is true, docker_tls_verify must be set to the string '1'.

trigger_keywords: secret from file trimmed
file contents whitespace stripped
inline value kept as-is
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The secret resolver checks whether the configured value is a path to an existing file on disk. If the file exists, the secret value is replaced by the file contents with leading and trailing whitespace trimmed. If the value does not correspond to an existing file, the inline string is used exactly as provided without any modification.

trigger_keywords: scope absent rejected
scope mismatch behavior
scope present but wrong value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The scope selector requires both that the scope marker be present AND that its value exactly equal the configured target scope. A candidate with no scope assigned (present=false) is rejected. A candidate with a scope present but a different value is also rejected. Only an exact string match with present=true is admitted.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'filter', 'env_auth', 'parse_server', 'docker_env', 'secret_resolve'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: whatever token format the registry expects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Base64 encoding of a compact JSON string with fields in strict order: {"username": "...", "password": "..."} — as demonstrated by the expected output in feature2_1_env_credentials.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: translate our connection settings into the right env vars for the Docker client
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3.1: map host->DOCKER_HOST (output as docker_host=), tlsverify->DOCKER_TLS_VERIFY (output as docker_tls_verify=, value '1' when true, empty string when false/absent), with default host unix:///var/run/docker.sock

Feature 1.4 scope selector: candidate must have scope present=true AND scope value exactly equal to the configured target scope string; used as an optional layer in the Feature 1.5 composite selector when scope parameter is non-empty

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output behavior, and refer to the feature descriptions in start.md for the business rules. If it's about how pieces combine, look at the composite filter (feature1_5) as the reference integration example.

feature1_1_filter_by_name.json.description: Selecting containers by an explicit allow-list of names. A name selector is built from a list of target names layered on top of a base selector that admits everything. A candidate is admitted only when its name equals one of the target names, where the comparison also succeeds against the candidate name with its first character removed (to tolerate a leading path separator on the reported name). When the target name list is empty the selector imposes no name restriction and the base selector decides alone (admitting every candidate). Output lists each candidate as its name and whether it was admitted.

feature1_2_filter_by_enable_label.json.description: Restricting selection to containers that explicitly carry an opt-in enable marker. Each candidate exposes an enable state as a pair: a boolean value plus a presence flag indicating whether the marker was set at all. The selector admits a candidate only when the marker is present, regardless of whether its value is true or false; when the marker is absent the candidate is rejected. Output lists each candidate as its name and whether it was admitted.

feature1_3_filter_by_disabled_label.json.description: Excluding only containers that explicitly opt out via the enable marker. Each candidate exposes an enable state as a pair: a boolean value plus a presence flag. The selector rejects a candidate only when the marker is present and its value is the opt-out value (false); in every other situation (marker present and true, or marker absent entirely) the candidate is admitted. Output lists each candidate as its name and whether it was admitted.

feature1_4_filter_by_scope.json.description: Restricting selection to containers that belong to a specific named scope. A scope selector is built from a target scope string. Each candidate exposes its scope as a pair: a string value plus a presence flag indicating whether a scope was assigned. The selector admits a candidate only when the candidate has a scope present and that scope equals the target scope; a mismatched scope or an absent scope causes rejection. Output lists each candidate as its name and whether it was admitted.

feature1_5_composite_filter.json.description: The composite selector used to choose update targets, assembled from several layers: an explicit name allow-list, an optional requirement that the opt-in enable marker be present, an optional scope restriction, and an always-applied exclusion of containers whose enable marker explicitly opts out. The opt-out exclusion is evaluated first, so a container that explicitly opts out is rejected before any other layer is consulted. Each candidate exposes a name and an enable state (value plus presence flag). This case configures the selector with a name allow-list only. Output lists each candidate as its name and whether it survived the full chain.

cases.expected_output:
container=Invalid included=false
container=test included=true
container=Invalid included=false
container=test included=true
container=x included=false

feature1_6_base_predicates.json.description: The two base selection predicates that the larger selectors are built upon. One is an unconditional admit-all predicate that accepts every candidate regardless of its attributes. The other is a self-identification predicate that admits a candidate only when it reports itself as an instance of the update agent. Each candidate reports a name plus a self-identification flag. Output lists each candidate as its name and whether it was admitted.

feature2_1_env_credentials.json.description: Building an encoded registry credential token from credentials supplied through the environment. When both a user name and a password are provided through their respective environment variables, the toolkit assembles an auth object and emits its transport encoding: the Base64 of a compact JSON document carrying the user name and password fields. When either the user name or the password is missing, no token can be produced and a neutral missing-credentials error category is reported instead. Output is either the encoded token or the neutral error line.

feature2_2_server_address.json.description: Extracting the registry server component from a container image reference. The reference is first parsed; the server component is then taken to be the substring up to the first path separator of the parsed reference. A fully qualified reference whose leading component is a host name yields that host name; a reference whose leading component is an organization (no host) yields that organization name. An input that cannot be parsed as a reference (for example, the empty string) reports a neutral invalid-reference error category. Output is either the extracted server component or the neutral error line.

feature3_1_docker_env.json.description: Translating command-line connection options into the environment variables that configure the daemon API client. Three options may be supplied: a daemon host address, a boolean verify-TLS switch, and an API version. With no options supplied the host falls back to its built-in default socket address and the verify-TLS variable is left unset (empty). When a custom host is supplied it is propagated verbatim, and when the verify-TLS switch is enabled the corresponding variable is set to the string flag value. Output reports the resolved host and the resolved verify-TLS variable.

feature3_2_secret_from_file.json.description: Resolving a sensitive option value that may be supplied either inline or as a reference to a file holding the secret. The resolver inspects the configured value: if it names an existing file on disk, the value is replaced by the trimmed contents of that file; otherwise the inline string is kept as-is. The first case supplies the secret directly as a literal string. The second case supplies the secret indirectly through a file whose contents become the resolved value. Output reports the resolved secret value.
